heroOverview: Trimethylaminuria (ICD-10: E72.52) is an inherited metabolic disorder in which the liver enzyme flavin-containing monooxygenase 3 (FMO3) fails to convert odorous trimethylamine (TMA) into its odourless oxide, causing TMA to accumulate and be excreted through sweat, urine, and breath. The result is a persistent fish-like body odour that waxes and wanes with diet and hormonal cycles. Prevalence estimates range from 1 in 200,000 to as many as 1 in 1,000 when mild heterozygous cases are included. The condition does not damage organs but causes profound psychosocial distress: surveys report depression and social isolation in the majority of affected individuals. Management centres on a low-choline diet, targeted supplementary strategies, and psychological support.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 1 in 200,000 for classic biallelic disease; heterozygous cases may affect up to 1 in 1,000 (NIH Genetic and Rare Diseases Information Center)
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ethnicities; symptoms more pronounced in women, particularly before and during menstruation and around menopause
keyStats.avgAge: Classically identified in early childhood after weaning; mild acquired forms can appear at any age
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global burden unknown; may be substantially under-diagnosed due to embarrassment and physician unfamiliarity

definition: Trimethylaminuria (ICD-10: E72.52), often called fish odour syndrome, is a disorder of choline and TMA metabolism caused by reduced activity of the FMO3 enzyme in the liver. FMO3 normally oxidises TMA — produced in the gut when bacteria metabolise dietary choline, lecithin, carnitine, and TMAO from fish — into odourless TMA-N-oxide. When FMO3 activity is diminished or absent, free TMA accumulates in the body and is secreted in sweat, urine, vaginal secretions, and expired air, producing a characteristic fishy or rotting smell. Most classic cases are autosomal recessive, caused by biallelic loss-of-function variants in the FMO3 gene on chromosome 1q24. Heterozygous carriers may have milder odour, and acquired or secondary trimethylaminuria can arise from liver disease, excessive dietary choline load, gut dysbiosis, or hormonal factors that suppress FMO3 expression. Severity fluctuates with diet, menstrual cycle, fever, exercise, and stress. There is no organ toxicity; all consequences derive from the social and psychological impact of the odour.

typesClassification.description: Both FMO3 alleles carry loss-of-function variants; FMO3 enzyme activity is severely reduced or absent. Persistent fishy odour from childhood that intensifies with choline-rich foods.
One functional FMO3 allele; partial enzyme activity. Odour is typically intermittent and triggered by large choline loads or hormonal fluctuation.
FMO3 activity is reduced by liver disease, gut dysbiosis, or excessive dietary precursor intake in the absence of a genetic variant; resolves when the underlying cause is addressed.
Women with otherwise normal FMO3 genotype experience transient fishy odour premenstrually and around menopause, when endogenous hormones down-regulate FMO3 expression.

primarySymptoms: A persistent or intermittent fishy, rotting-fish, or rubbish-like body odour that is most noticeable to others nearby and is not eliminated by normal washing.
Odour emitted from sweat, which is often the first and most socially disruptive route of excretion, intensifying with physical activity, heat, or anxiety.
Fishy-smelling urine that may be noticed by the patient or by others sharing a bathroom, especially after eating fish, eggs, or legumes.
Malodorous breath (halitosis) with a fishy character that persists despite tooth brushing and mouthwash use.
Vaginal odour with a fishy quality in women, often worse before menstruation and mistaken for bacterial vaginosis.
Worsening of all odour symptoms after eating choline-rich foods including eggs, offal, fish, legumes, and soy products.
Cyclical intensification of odour in women around menstruation, during pregnancy, and in the perimenopause, reflecting hormonal suppression of FMO3.
Psychological symptoms — depression, social anxiety, social isolation, and relationship difficulties — as a direct consequence of the odour rather than any neurological effect of TMA.
Occupational and academic difficulties arising from avoidance of close-contact work environments and persistent concern about others noticing the smell.
Episodic rather than constant odour in heterozygous carriers, with symptom-free periods between dietary or hormonal triggers.
earlyWarningSigns: Parents noticing a fishy smell from a young child that is unrelated to their diet.
Persistent body odour in an adolescent that does not respond to regular hygiene and deodorants.
Family members or close contacts mentioning an unusual smell that the person themselves may not detect.
A fishy urine odour that appears predictably after eating eggs or fish and then fades.
emergencySigns: Trimethylaminuria itself is not a medical emergency; there are no acute metabolic crises.
Severe depression, self-harm ideation, or suicidal thoughts require immediate psychiatric evaluation and represent the most serious complication of this condition.
Newly acquired fishy odour with jaundice, dark urine, and right upper quadrant pain may indicate acute liver disease rather than genetic trimethylaminuria and warrants urgent assessment.

causes.description: Loss-of-function mutations in both copies of the FMO3 gene on chromosome 1q24 eliminate or severely reduce the liver enzyme responsible for oxidising TMA, allowing the malodorous compound to accumulate. Over 40 pathogenic variants have been identified, with Y238H, E158K, and P153L among the most prevalent in European populations.

Intestinal microbiota ferment dietary choline, phosphatidylcholine, lecithin, L-carnitine, and TMAO from marine fish into TMA. In a person with intact FMO3, this TMA is promptly oxidised in the liver; in trimethylaminuria, it circulates systemically and is excreted through body surfaces.

Foods rich in choline (eggs, liver, kidney, legumes, soy, wheat germ) and marine-derived TMAO (saltwater fish) dramatically raise the TMA substrate available to gut bacteria, directly amplifying odour severity. The relationship is near-linear: reducing precursor intake proportionally reduces odour.

Oestrogen and progesterone metabolites, particularly at premenstrual peaks and during perimenopause, directly down-regulate hepatic FMO3 expression in women. This creates cyclical odour in carriers and worsens symptoms in biallelic disease during hormonal fluctuation.

Because FMO3 is predominantly hepatic, significant hepatocellular damage from any cause — cirrhosis, hepatitis, fatty liver disease — can impair TMA oxidation sufficiently to produce secondary trimethylaminuria independent of the FMO3 genotype.

Altered intestinal microbial communities can produce excess TMA from normal dietary choline loads, overwhelming even partially functional FMO3 capacity and triggering or worsening symptoms. Antibiotic courses that reduce TMA-producing bacteria can transiently improve odour.

riskFactors.description: Autosomal recessive inheritance; carriers of two loss-of-function FMO3 alleles have the condition regardless of diet or lifestyle, although severity is modulated by diet and hormones.

Approximately 1% of some populations carry a single FMO3 variant. These individuals may experience mild or intermittent odour, particularly after large choline meals, during menstruation, or in the perimenopause.
Frequent consumption of eggs, offal, legumes, and saltwater fish substantially increases TMA substrate delivery to gut bacteria, amplifying odour severity in proportion to dietary load.
Women with trimethylaminuria consistently report worse odour premenstrually, during pregnancy, and in the perimenopause, mirroring FMO3 suppression by oestrogen and progesterone metabolites.
A microbiome rich in TMA-producing bacteria (e.g. certain Clostridiales and Erysipelotrichaceae) generates more TMA from equivalent dietary substrates, increasing baseline odour.
Hepatic dysfunction from any cause reduces effective FMO3 activity. Managing underlying liver disease can partially restore TMA-oxidising capacity.
Parents who are biological relatives have a higher chance of both carrying the same recessive FMO3 variant, raising the probability of biallelic offspring.

affectedDemographics: Children diagnosed in infancy or early childhood after weaning onto a choline-containing diet
Women of reproductive age, who experience cyclical symptom intensification
Perimenopausal and postmenopausal women, in whom hormonal changes worsen TMA excretion
Adults of all ethnicities; no ethnic group is spared, though specific FMO3 variants differ by population
Heterozygous carriers (estimated 1 in 100 in some populations) who have mild or sporadic symptoms
Individuals with chronic liver disease who develop secondary trimethylaminuria regardless of genotype
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis requires a combination of clinical recognition, dietary challenge, urinary TMA quantification, and molecular genetic testing. The condition is widely under-diagnosed because many clinicians are unfamiliar with it, patients are ashamed to report the odour, and standard investigations are normal. A detailed dietary history exploring choline-rich food intake and its correlation with odour fluctuation is the essential starting point. The definitive biochemical test is measurement of free TMA and TMA-N-oxide in a spot or 24-hour urine sample; an elevated ratio of free TMA to total TMA (TMA plus TMAO) above approximately 15% after a choline challenge (usually 5 g of choline bitartrate) confirms reduced FMO3 activity. In classic biallelic disease the ratio typically exceeds 30-50% even at baseline. FMO3 gene sequencing identifies the causative variants and confirms the genetic diagnosis, distinguishing primary from secondary trimethylaminuria and determining carrier status in family members. Liver function tests are useful to exclude significant hepatic disease as a secondary cause. Thyroid function and hormonal assays can contextualise cyclical odour in women. The key differential diagnoses are bacterial vaginosis in women, other metabolic odour disorders (isovaleric acidaemia, maple syrup urine disease), and social stigma from ordinary body odour that is culturally perceived as excessive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the proportion of free odorous TMA relative to its odourless oxide in urine to confirm reduced FMO3 activity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You will collect a urine sample — ideally after eating a choline-rich meal or following a formal choline challenge. A ratio above approximately 15% of total TMA is abnormal; classic disease typically shows ratios above 30%. The sample is sent to a specialist metabolic laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardises the TMA precursor load to unmask mild or intermittent FMO3 deficiency, particularly in heterozygous carriers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You take an oral dose of choline bitartrate (typically 5 g), then collect urine over the next 4-8 hours. The ratio of free to total urinary TMA is measured; an elevated result confirms reduced FMO3 activity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies pathogenic variants to confirm genetic diagnosis, distinguish primary from secondary trimethylaminuria, and enable cascade testing of family members.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood sample is taken from a vein in the arm. DNA is extracted and sequenced in a specialist genetics laboratory; results may take several weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes significant hepatocellular damage as a secondary cause of reduced FMO3 activity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood test measuring ALT, AST, bilirubin, albumin, and alkaline phosphatase; results are available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the relationship between choline-rich food intake and odour severity to guide diagnosis and personalise dietary restriction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You record everything you eat and rate odour intensity over a two-week period; a consistent pattern of worsening after eggs, fish, or legumes strongly supports the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the abundance of TMA-producing bacterial species, useful in secondary trimethylaminuria or when gut-targeted therapies are being considered.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A stool sample is sent to a specialist laboratory for 16S rRNA sequencing; a commercial test is sufficient for this purpose, and results are available in one to two weeks.

treatmentOverview: There is currently no cure for trimethylaminuria, but a combination of dietary modification, intermittent antibiotic courses, and targeted adjuncts can substantially reduce odour intensity. The foundation of management is a low-choline diet that restricts eggs, offal, legumes, soy, saltwater fish, and wheat germ, which directly reduces the TMA substrate available to gut bacteria. Many patients achieve meaningful improvement by identifying and avoiding their personal high-trigger foods through a dietary diary. Short courses of neomycin, metronidazole, or rifaximin reduce the gut bacteria responsible for TMA production and can provide weeks of relief; because resistance and side-effects limit long-term use, these are used intermittently at times of highest social importance rather than continuously. Activated charcoal and copper chlorophyllin have been used as oral adsorbents to bind TMA in the gut before absorption; evidence is limited but some patients report modest benefit. Riboflavin (vitamin B2) supplementation at doses of 30-60 mg three times daily may enhance residual FMO3 activity in some individuals, particularly those with the P153L or E158K variants. Acidic soaps and washes (pH 5.5-6.5) reduce skin surface TMA — which is a weak base — and therefore diminish its volatilisation from sweat. Women whose symptoms worsen premenstrually may benefit from continuous combined hormonal contraception to smooth the hormonal cycle. Psychological support and, where indicated, treatment for depression or anxiety, are as important as any biochemical intervention and should be offered proactively.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-choline dietary restriction
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of management; reducing dietary intake of choline precursors (eggs, offal, legumes, soy, saltwater fish) lowers TMA substrate delivery to gut bacteria. Patients work with a specialist dietitian to achieve adequate protein and micronutrient intake while minimising choline.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dietary restriction reduces urinary TMA excretion by approximately 30-50% in most patients, with greater reduction when adherence is strict (based on case series data from specialist metabolic centres).

medicalTreatments.name: Short-course gut-active antibiotics (neomycin, metronidazole, rifaximin)
medicalTreatments.description: Brief courses (1-2 weeks) of poorly absorbed antibiotics reduce the abundance of TMA-producing gut bacteria. Used intermittently — for example before social events or during menstruation — rather than continuously to limit resistance and adverse effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case reports and small series show odour reduction in 60-80% of patients during and for several weeks after antibiotic courses; no large randomised trials exist.

medicalTreatments.name: Riboflavin (vitamin B2) supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Riboflavin at 30-60 mg three times daily is a cofactor for FMO3 and may boost residual enzyme activity in patients with certain hypomorphic FMO3 variants. It is safe, inexpensive, and worth a trial in all patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Biochemical improvement in urinary TMA ratio reported in approximately 50% of patients with hypomorphic variants; less evidence in null-mutation disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral activated charcoal or copper chlorophyllin
medicalTreatments.description: These adsorbents bind TMA in the gut lumen before it is absorbed, reducing systemic and therefore secreted TMA. Copper chlorophyllin 60 mg three times daily has been reported to halve urinary TMA in one small controlled study.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Copper chlorophyllin reduced urinary TMA excretion by approximately 50% in a published case study; activated charcoal evidence is anecdotal. Prolonged use of activated charcoal risks nutrient malabsorption.

medicalTreatments.name: Laxatives to reduce gut transit time
medicalTreatments.description: Shortening the time food spends in the colon reduces the period available for gut bacterial TMA fermentation. A mild osmotic laxative or high-fibre approach is used adjunctively rather than as a primary treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No controlled evidence; used as a supportive measure that may reduce TMA production by an estimated 10-20% based on theoretical transit-time modelling.

medicalTreatments.name: Psychological therapy and antidepressants
medicalTreatments.description: Because depression and social anxiety affect the majority of patients, cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and, where clinically indicated, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) or other antidepressants are a core part of comprehensive management.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: CBT for health-related stigma and social anxiety disorder has response rates above 60% in the general anxiety literature; specific trimethylaminuria data are limited to case reports.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acidic skin washes
alternativeTreatments.description: Using soap or body wash with a pH of 5.5-6.5 reduces skin-surface TMA volatilisation by keeping it in its less volatile protonated form. Patients report this as a practical adjunct to other measures.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotic supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Modifying the gut microbiome towards TMA non-producing species is theoretically attractive; lactobacillus-containing probiotics have been used empirically by some patients, though robust evidence from controlled trials is lacking.

alternativeTreatments.name: Continuous combined hormonal contraception
alternativeTreatments.description: Women whose odour reliably worsens premenstrually may benefit from eliminating the hormonal cycle with a continuous-dose combined oral contraceptive pill, reviewed with their gynaecologist.

whySeeSpecialist: Trimethylaminuria requires input from a metabolic physician or biochemical genetics specialist to confirm the diagnosis, perform FMO3 sequencing, and oversee urinary TMA testing, which is not available in routine labs. A specialist dietitian experienced in metabolic conditions is needed to design a nutritionally adequate low-choline diet. Psychological support from a mental health professional familiar with stigmatising chronic conditions should be integrated from the start of care.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a metabolic disease specialist or clinical geneticist with experience in amino acid and organic acid disorders, ideally in a centre that offers FMO3 molecular testing and urinary organic acid analysis. A paired referral to a specialist metabolic dietitian is essential. The British Inherited Metabolic Disease Group (BIMDG) and the Society for Inherited Metabolic Disorders (SIMD) maintain directories of specialist centres. Ask whether the centre can perform the choline loading test in-house or refers to an accredited external laboratory.
preventionStrategies: Maintain a consistently low-choline diet by avoiding eggs, offal, legumes, soy-based foods, and saltwater fish or using them only in small portions.
Use a food diary to identify personal trigger foods and track odour severity, which helps fine-tune dietary restrictions.
For women, discuss continuous combined hormonal contraception with a gynaecologist to reduce cyclical odour intensification.
Use a mildly acidic soap (pH 5.5-6.5) for all body washing to reduce skin-surface TMA volatilisation.
Consider periodic short antibiotic courses (under medical supervision) before high-stakes social events rather than waiting until odour is unmanageable.
Offer cascade genetic testing to siblings and children of affected individuals so carriers are identified and can make informed dietary and reproductive choices.
lifestyleModifications: Carry a small list of high-choline foods to consult when eating out or reading ingredient labels.
Choose plant-based protein sources low in choline — lean poultry, white rice, pasta, most fruits and vegetables — as dietary staples.
Shower immediately after exercise, and keep spare clothing available when possible, to minimise accumulation of odorous sweat.
Wear breathable, moisture-wicking fabrics that reduce sweat accumulation on the skin.
Prepare for social situations proactively: plan meals the day before important events and consider an antibiotic course if prescribed for this purpose.
Join a patient community such as the TMAU Support Group or online forums, where validated coping strategies are shared by people with lived experience.
Tell a trusted colleague or friend — having an ally who understands the condition reduces the psychological burden and removes the fear of being 'discovered'.

recommended: Lean white-meat poultry in moderate portions as a lower-choline protein source
White rice, pasta, bread, and other refined grains, which contribute little choline
Most fruits and non-cruciferous vegetables, which are inherently low in choline
Moderate amounts of low-fat dairy products, which have less choline than whole eggs
Olive oil and other plant oils as cooking fat alternatives to butter
avoid: Eggs, especially yolk, which contain around 115 mg choline per large egg — the single most potent trigger
Offal and organ meats (liver, kidney), which are very high in choline
Saltwater fish and shellfish, which contain both choline and TMA-N-oxide that gut bacteria convert to TMA
Legumes — lentils, chickpeas, kidney beans, and soy products — all significant choline sources
Wheat germ and nutritional yeast, which contain concentrated choline
High-dose choline or lecithin supplements, including some prenatal vitamins

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is encouraged for general health and psychological wellbeing. Because physical exertion increases sweating, which is a major route of TMA excretion, patients should plan for showering immediately afterwards and carry a change of clothes. Outdoor exercise or well-ventilated spaces minimise odour perception by others. Exercise before meals rather than after a choline-containing meal reduces the amount of circulating TMA available for sweat excretion at the time of peak activity.
dailyManagement: Check ingredient labels on all packaged foods for egg, lecithin, choline, or soy before purchasing.
Record meals and rate odour intensity each evening to identify emerging trigger foods.
Shower with an acidic (pH 5.5-6.5) body wash at least once daily, and immediately after physical activity.
Take riboflavin supplements with meals if prescribed, as a consistent daily habit.
Plan the following day's meals in advance to avoid inadvertent choline loading.
Keep a supply of the prescribed antibiotic course available for use at high-stakes social or professional events, under your doctor's guidance.
Schedule regular reviews with your metabolic dietitian — at least every 6 months — to reassess nutritional adequacy and adjust the diet.
prognosis: Trimethylaminuria is a lifelong condition with no curative treatment currently available, but long-term organ damage does not occur. With consistent dietary adherence, riboflavin supplementation, and appropriate use of gut-decontaminating antibiotics, many patients achieve a substantial reduction in odour intensity and lead normal personal and professional lives. The greatest long-term burden is psychosocial: untreated depression and social isolation are common and can be more disabling than the odour itself. Proactive mental health support significantly improves quality of life and functional outcomes. Ongoing research into gut microbiome modulation and FMO3 gene therapy offers potential for more definitive treatments in the future. Women often find that symptom severity decreases after menopause once hormonal fluctuation settles.
recoveryTimeline: There is no cure-related timeline; management is lifelong. Dietary restriction reduces odour within 24-48 hours of removing high-choline foods. A course of neomycin or metronidazole typically produces odour improvement within 3-7 days, with the benefit lasting several weeks after the course ends. Riboflavin supplementation may take 4-6 weeks to reach maximum effect. Psychological outcomes with CBT typically show measurable improvement over 8-16 weekly sessions.
complications: Major depressive disorder, affecting the majority of patients in survey studies, driven by shame, stigma, and social isolation.
Social anxiety disorder and agoraphobia, arising from fear of others noticing the odour in close-contact settings.
Academic or occupational underachievement due to avoidance of school, workplaces, or public environments.
Relationship difficulties including social withdrawal, avoidance of intimacy, and relationship breakdown.
Nutritional deficiency from overly restrictive choline avoidance without dietitian supervision, particularly choline itself — an essential nutrient — and protein.

supportResources.name: TMAU Support — MEBO Research
supportResources.description: International patient advocacy and research organisation specifically focused on metabolic body odour conditions including trimethylaminuria.

supportResources.name: NIH Genetic and Rare Diseases Information Center — Trimethylaminuria
supportResources.description: Authoritative plain-English overview of the condition, diagnostic criteria, and research resources from the U.S. National Institutes of Health.

supportResources.name: NORD — National Organization for Rare Disorders
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information, support group links, and specialist centre directories for trimethylaminuria.

sources.title: Phillips IR, Shephard EA. Trimethylaminuria. In: GeneReviews [Internet]. NCBI Bookshelf.
Messenger J et al. Fish odour syndrome: a case study including psychiatric aspects. Psychol Health Med, 2013.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bromhidrosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bromhidrosis is a skin condition producing malodorous sweat from bacterial decomposition of apocrine secretions; it lacks the fishy character of trimethylaminuria and is not linked to dietary choline or FMO3 deficiency.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute intermittent porphyria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Porphyria causes periodic abdominal crises, neurological symptoms, and discoloured urine but does not cause a fishy body odour; both are rare metabolic enzyme disorders but affect different pathways.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression is a primary psychiatric diagnosis; in trimethylaminuria depression is secondary to social stigma from the odour, and the underlying metabolic cause is missed when only the psychiatric presentation is treated.

relatedConditions.name: Bromhidrosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A related cause of socially distressing body odour arising from skin bacterial activity rather than systemic TMA excess; the two conditions are sometimes confused.

relatedConditions.name: Acute intermittent porphyria
relatedConditions.relevance: Another inherited metabolic enzyme disorder affecting an hepatic pathway, relevant in the differential diagnosis of metabolic causes of abnormal odour or discoloured secretions.

relatedConditions.name: Coeliac disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Gut inflammatory conditions can alter microbiome composition and increase TMA production from normal dietary loads, potentially triggering or worsening secondary trimethylaminuria.

faqs.question: What is trimethylaminuria?
faqs.answer: Trimethylaminuria is a metabolic disorder where the liver enzyme FMO3 cannot convert odorous trimethylamine (TMA) into its odourless form, causing a persistent fishy body odour in sweat, urine, and breath. It is genetic in most cases.

faqs.question: What does trimethylaminuria smell like?
faqs.answer: The odour is described as fishy, rotting fish, or rubbish-like. It comes from sweat, urine, breath, and vaginal secretions and intensifies after eating eggs, fish, or legumes. Many patients cannot detect their own smell because they become habituated to it.

faqs.question: How is trimethylaminuria diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by measuring the ratio of free TMA to total TMA in urine, ideally after a choline challenge. A ratio above roughly 15% is abnormal. FMO3 gene sequencing then identifies the causative variant. Standard blood and urine tests are normal.

faqs.question: Is trimethylaminuria curable?
faqs.answer: There is currently no cure. However, a low-choline diet, short antibiotic courses, riboflavin supplementation, and acidic skin washes can substantially reduce odour intensity. Research into gene therapy and gut microbiome modulation is ongoing.

faqs.question: Which foods should I avoid with trimethylaminuria?
faqs.answer: Avoid eggs, offal, saltwater fish, legumes (lentils, chickpeas, soy), wheat germ, and choline or lecithin supplements. These foods deliver the most TMA precursor to gut bacteria. A specialist dietitian can help you maintain adequate nutrition while avoiding triggers.

faqs.question: Does trimethylaminuria affect lifespan or cause organ damage?
faqs.answer: Trimethylaminuria does not damage organs or shorten lifespan. All physical harm is absent; the burden is entirely psychosocial — depression, social isolation, and relationship difficulties, which can be severe but are treatable.

faqs.question: Why does trimethylaminuria smell worse around my period?
faqs.answer: Oestrogen and progesterone metabolites at premenstrual peaks directly suppress FMO3 enzyme activity in the liver, allowing more TMA to escape oxidation. This hormonal effect is well documented and explains why many women find symptoms reliably worse before menstruation.

faqs.question: Can antibiotics help with trimethylaminuria?
faqs.answer: Short courses of gut-active antibiotics such as neomycin, metronidazole, or rifaximin reduce TMA-producing intestinal bacteria and provide odour relief for several weeks. They are used intermittently for important social or professional events, not taken continuously.

faqs.question: Is trimethylaminuria inherited?
faqs.answer: Classic trimethylaminuria is autosomal recessive, meaning a child must inherit a defective FMO3 gene from both parents to develop the condition. Carriers with one copy often have mild intermittent odour. A genetic counsellor can advise families on inheritance risk.

faqs.question: Can I pass trimethylaminuria to my children?
faqs.answer: If both parents carry a pathogenic FMO3 variant, each pregnancy has a 1 in 4 (25%) chance of the child inheriting two defective copies and having the condition. Preconception genetic counselling and prenatal testing are available at specialist centres.

faqs.question: Does riboflavin help with fish odour syndrome?
faqs.answer: Riboflavin (vitamin B2) at 30-60 mg three times daily may boost residual FMO3 enzyme activity in patients with certain hypomorphic gene variants, reducing urinary TMA excretion. It is safe and worth a trial in all patients, even though it helps about half.

faqs.question: How common is trimethylaminuria?
faqs.answer: Classic biallelic trimethylaminuria affects roughly 1 in 200,000 people. When mild heterozygous cases are included, estimates rise to as high as 1 in 1,000. The condition is significantly under-diagnosed due to embarrassment, physician unfamiliarity, and normal standard tests.

faqs.question: Will the smell go away after menopause?
faqs.answer: Many women with trimethylaminuria report that symptom intensity decreases after menopause once the cyclical hormonal fluctuation that suppresses FMO3 expression settles. It does not eliminate the underlying deficiency, but removes a major modulating factor.

faqs.question: Does washing help reduce the odour?
faqs.answer: Regular washing helps but does not eliminate trimethylaminuria odour because TMA is continuously secreted from the body. Using an acidic soap or body wash at pH 5.5-6.5 is more effective than alkaline soaps because it keeps TMA in its less volatile protonated form.

faqs.question: Can stress or anxiety make trimethylaminuria worse?
faqs.answer: Emotional stress increases sweating, which is the primary route of TMA excretion; more sweating means more odour. Anxiety about the odour itself further increases sweating, creating a feedback loop. Managing stress through therapy and relaxation techniques is an important part of care.

faqs.question: Is there a support group for trimethylaminuria?
faqs.answer: Yes. MEBO Research (meboresearch.org) is the main international organisation for metabolic body odour conditions including trimethylaminuria. Online communities and forums provide peer support and practical management strategies shared by people with lived experience.

faqs.question: Can a child have trimethylaminuria without any family history?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both parents may carry a single FMO3 variant without symptoms or awareness, since carriers are often unaffected or only mildly affected. The child inheriting both copies then develops the condition with no apparent family history of it.

faqs.question: Does trimethylaminuria affect mental health?
faqs.answer: Yes, profoundly. Studies show depression and social anxiety in the majority of affected individuals, driven by shame and fear of social judgment. Proactive mental health support — including CBT and, when needed, antidepressants — is as important as dietary management.

faqs.question: What does a specialist do for trimethylaminuria?
faqs.answer: A metabolic specialist confirms the diagnosis with urinary TMA testing and FMO3 gene sequencing, prescribes riboflavin and antibiotic courses, coordinates dietitian input for choline restriction, and refers to mental health services. Specialist centres also monitor nutritional adequacy.

faqs.question: Can trimethylaminuria be managed with diet alone?
faqs.answer: Diet is the foundation of management and can reduce TMA excretion by 30-50%, but most patients need additional measures — riboflavin, intermittent antibiotics, and acidic washes — to achieve satisfactory odour control. Dietitian supervision is essential to prevent choline and protein deficiency.

metaDescription: Trimethylaminuria causes a persistent fishy body odour from a liver enzyme defect. Learn about FMO3 deficiency, diagnosis, diet, and treatment options.
